lesson.menu_title: Red Flag -- Raising a Risk Before It Explodes
lesson.menu_desc: Raise a risk out loud: likelihood, impact, mitigation, decision -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Red Flag -- Raising a Risk Before It Explodes
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 13 -- Red Flag

plan_row.tema: Red Flag -- Raising a Risk Before It Explodes
plan_row.foco: Probability (may / might / could / it is likely that) + mitigation (unless / to mitigate)
plan_row.atividade: Risk card + role-play with a stakeholder who downplays the risk
plan_row.homework: Record a 2-min risk warning: likelihood, impact, mitigation, decision

bank.label: The five lines of a red flag
items: Before we plan the sprint, I want to flag one risk.
The client's security team has been silent for eleven days.
It is likely that the approval takes four more weeks.
Worst case, the go-live moves to August.
To mitigate it, we start the tests on the old database this week.
I need one decision from you by Friday.
Two developers leave the project in June. Nobody has replaced them, and nobody is worried.
The vendor's test environment has failed twice this month. The go-live depends on it.
The client wants a new feature in the last sprint. Everyone says it is small.

items.situation: Open the meeting with the risk, in one sentence.
tips: Say it before the agenda, not after.

items.situation: Give the warning sign -- a fact, with a number.
tips: Days of silence, failures, people who left.

items.situation: Say how likely it is, and why.
tips: 'It is likely that..., because last year...'

items.situation: Your director says: 'You are being dramatic.' Answer.
tips: Do not defend your feelings -- repeat the number.
'Eleven days of silence, four weeks last year.'

items.situation: Give the mitigation, this week -- not in July.
tips: Something you can start on Monday.

items.situation: Ask for the decision, with a deadline.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: If this is so serious, why is nobody else worried?
Are you covering yourself, or are you protecting the project?
What do you want me to tell the client -- that we are not ready?

pressure.text: Answer first, number second, plan third. Never say "problem" -- say risk, likelihood, and decision.

listenings.text: Diogo, before the steering meeting. I know you want to talk about the database approval. Let me tell you how this room works. If you say the word problem, everybody stops listening and starts defending themselves. So do not bring me a problem. Bring me a risk, with three numbers: how likely it is, what it costs us, and what you want to do about it. And bring a decision. If you just say the client is slow, I will say what everybody says: let us wait and see. Give me something to decide, and give me a date. If you do not, in five weeks this is your fault, not theirs.

Before we plan the sprint, I want to flag one risk. The client's security team has not approved the new database, and they have been silent for eleven days. That is the warning sign. It is likely that the approval takes four more weeks, because that is what it took last year. The impact: the migration cannot start, and the go-live moves. Worst case, we lose the whole of July. This is a dependency, and it is not in my control -- but I can mitigate it. My plan is simple. This week, we start the tests on the old database, so we do not lose time. And I need one decision from you today: do we go live with the old database in June, or do we wait for the approval and move the date? Both are acceptable. Doing nothing is not.

extra_audio.text: Diogo opens the meeting and flags one risk: the security team has been silent for eleven days. Sophia tells him not to be dramatic. He says how likely it is: four more weeks, like last year. He gives the worst case, and the mitigation: start the tests on the old database. Finally, he asks for one decision by Friday.
